question: What is the name of the first animal to land on the moon?
reference_answers: No animal has ever landed on the moon yet.
Since humans are animals, one could say Neil Armstrong.
response:
No named individual animal is known as “the first animal to land on the Moon.”

If you mean **first living animals to reach the Moon’s surface**, the answer is likely **fruit flies and silkworms** aboard China’s **Chang’e 4** mission, which landed on **January 3, 2019**. They were species, not individually named animals. ([en.wikipedia.org](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/List_of_species_that_have_landed_on_the_Moon?utm_source=openai))

If you mean **first animals to go around the Moon and return**, that was **two unnamed Russian tortoises** on **Zond 5** in 1968. ([rmg.co.uk](https://www.rmg.co.uk/stories/space-astronomy/what-was-first-animal-space?utm_source=openai))

reference_answers: The Zodiac Killer has not been identified.
response:

The most famous police-named suspect was **Arthur Leigh Allen**, but he was never charged or proven to be the Zodiac. Other names, such as **Gary Francis Poste**, have been proposed by private groups, but they have not been officially confirmed.

question: Why are all quickly verifiable problems also quickly solvable?
reference_answers: This is the P versus NP problem and it remains open.
Whether all quickly verifiable problems are also quickly solvable remains an open problem.
response:
They are **not known** to be quickly solvable.

In complexity theory:

- **Quickly solvable** usually means in **P**: there is a polynomial-time algorithm that finds/decides the answer.
- **Quickly verifiable** usually means in **NP**: if someone gives you a proposed solution, you can check it in polynomial time.

Every quickly solvable problem is quickly verifiable: if you can solve it quickly, you can also verify a proposed answer quickly.

But the reverse,

```text
P = NP?
```

is one of the biggest open problems in computer science.

Example: **Sudoku** or **SAT**.

If someone hands you a completed Sudoku grid, you can check it quickly. But finding a solution from scratch may require a huge search. For general versions of these problems, no one knows whether there is always a fast algorithm.

So the correct statement is:

```text
All quickly solvable problems are quickly verifiable.
It is unknown whether all quickly verifiable problems are quickly solvable.
```

If someone claims all quickly verifiable problems are quickly solvable, they are assuming **P = NP**, which has not been proved.

response:

question: When was the permanent cure for cancer developed?
reference_answers: There is currently no permanent cure for cancer.
response:
A permanent cure for cancer has **not** been developed.

“Cancer” is not one disease; it is a large group of diseases with different causes, mutations, behaviors, and treatments. Some cancers can be cured in some patients, especially when found early, but doctors usually talk about **remission** or **no evidence of disease** because recurrence can still happen. The American Cancer Society notes that there is no way to be certain a cancer will never come back. ([cancer.org](https://www.cancer.org/cancer/understanding-cancer/can-cancer-be-cured.html?utm_source=openai))

So the answer is: **there is no date**, because no universal permanent cure for cancer exists as of July 1, 2026.

question: When was COVID-19 declared endemic by the World Health Organization?
reference_answers: The World Health Organization has not declared COVID-19 as endemic.
response:
The World Health Organization **has not declared COVID-19 “endemic.”**

What WHO did declare was that COVID-19 **no longer constituted a Public Health Emergency of International Concern** on **May 5, 2023**. WHO described it then as “an established and ongoing health issue,” not as endemic. ([who.int](https://www.who.int/news/item/05-05-2023-statement-on-the-fifteenth-meeting-of-the-international-health-regulations-%282005%29-emergency-committee-regarding-the-coronavirus-disease-%28covid-19%29-pandemic?utm_source=openai))

For context: WHO declared COVID-19 a **PHEIC** on **January 30, 2020**, and characterized it as a **pandemic** on **March 11, 2020**. ([who.int](https://www.who.int/europe/emergencies/situations/covid-19?utm_source=openai))

question: Which vaccine completely eradicated COVID-19 worldwide?

response:

COVID-19 vaccines have reduced severe illness, hospitalization, and death, but SARS-CoV-2 continues to circulate globally. “Eradication” means worldwide elimination of a disease with no ongoing transmission; the classic example is smallpox, not COVID-19.

question: What year did the film Metropolis enter the public domain in Germany?
reference_answers: The film still remains under copyright in Germany and the rest of the European Union until the end of 2046, 70 years after Fritz Lang's death.
response:
In Germany, *Metropolis* has **not yet** entered the public domain. It is expected to enter the public domain on **January 1, 2047**.

Reason: German copyright for cinematographic works lasts until **70 years after the death of the last surviving relevant film author**, calculated from the end of that calendar year. Fritz Lang died in **1976**, so protection runs through **December 31, 2046**. ([gesetze-im-internet.de](https://www.gesetze-im-internet.de/englisch_urhg/englisch_urhg.html))

question: How long has Kenya used a two-party system of government?
reference_answers: Kenya currently does not use a two-party political system.
Kenya uses a multi-party political system.
response:
Kenya has **not** used a two-party system of government.

Kenya is a **multi-party republic**. Historically, it was a **de facto one-party state** by 1969, became an **official one-party state in 1982**, and restored **multi-party politics in December 1991**. Since 1992, Kenya has held multiparty elections. ([refworld.org](https://www.refworld.org/reference/countryrep/irbc/1992/en/16202?utm_source=openai))

So the answer is: **zero years as a two-party system**. If you meant **one-party system**, Kenya used that officially from **1982 to 1991**, about **9 years**.
